risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Randomisation was conducted using pre-generated random permuted blocks (block sizes of four and six). A data manager at the York Trials Unit, who was completely independent of the research team, created the randomisation programme" Comment: This describes a proper random allocation sequence using pre-generated random permuted blocks, which is an explicit random method and is sufficient to judge low risk for random sequence generation.

_evidence.method_quote: Randomisation was conducted using pre-generated random permuted blocks (block sizes of four and six).
_evidence.method_summary: Participants were allocated using pre-generated random permuted blocks with block sizes of four and six.
context_clues: block sizes of four and six
allocate participants to one of the two treatment arms

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "After completing the baseline assessment, the research nurse telephoned a secure and remote randomisation service, located at the York Trials Unit (University of York, UK)." Comment: This is explicit central allocation via a secure remote randomisation service, which prevents the enrolling nurse from foreseeing the next assignment before enrolment. The independent data manager and immediate allocation after the telephone call further support adequate concealment.

_evidence.concealment_quote: After completing the baseline assessment, the research nurse telephoned a secure and remote randomisation service, located at the York Trials Unit (University of York, UK). Randomisation was conducted using pre-generated random permuted blocks (block sizes of four and six). A data manager at the York Trials Unit, who was completely independent of the research team, created the randomisation programme, which was used to allocate participants to one of the two treatment arms: either NPWT or standard care (SC).

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "staff and participants could not be blinded to treatment" Comment: The study explicitly states that both participants and treating staff were unblinded, and the outcomes included subjective measures such as wound healing, pain (VAS), and EQ-5D HR-QoL, which could plausibly be influenced by knowledge of allocation. Although a blinded outcome assessor was used for wound photographs, that addresses detection bias rather than performance bias, so the dominant performance-bias risk across study outcomes is high.

_evidence.blinding_quote: “Since assessment of wound healing is somewhat subjective and staff and participants could not be blinded to treatment, it would be important to conduct blinded outcome assessment in a full trial.”
_evidence.blinding_method: none for participants and treating staff; blinded outcome assessment used digital photographs with a “blinded outcome assessor (experienced Tissue Viability nurse not involved in the day-to-day conduct of the trial)”

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: “A blinded outcome assessor (experienced Tissue Viability nurse not involved in the day-to-day conduct of the trial) then assessed photographs for (1) assessment of healing, (2) threats to blinding and (3) picture quality.” Comment: Outcome assessment for healing was blinded, so the healing outcome itself was protected. However, the study also collected patient-reported outcomes, including ulcer-related pain (“This was plotted on a 10 cm visual analogue scale… by the research nurse”) and EQ-5D HR-QoL questionnaires completed by participants, and the report explicitly states that “staff and participants could not be blinded to treatment” and that “The pain VAS was not appropriate.” Because the patient is the assessor for self-reported outcomes and participants were unblinded, knowledge of allocation could influence measurement of these subjective outcomes. Under the worst-outcome rule, the domain is High risk overall despite blinded photographic assessment of healing.

_evidence.assessor_blinding_quote: “A blinded outcome assessor (experienced Tissue Viability nurse not involved in the day-to-day conduct of the trial) then assessed photographs for (1) assessment of healing, (2) threats to blinding and (3) picture quality.”

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "There was no attrition recorded during this trial." Comment: The paper reports that all 12 recruited participants were randomised (6 NPWT, 6 SC) and that the analysed outcome data were intact at the trial level; the described early exits were due to later recruitment/shorter available follow-up, not post-randomization loss, so there is no evidence of attrition bias on the outcomes of interest.

per_arm_flow.reasons: “There was no attrition recorded during this trial.” “From the 12 recruited participants, 5 (2 NPWT and 3 SC participants) exited the trial after completing the maximum 6-month follow-up. A further five participants (two NPWT and three SC) completed less than the 6-month follow-up as they were recruited later into the study. Two participants died during the trial...”

“There was no attrition recorded during this trial.” “From the 12 recruited participants, 5 (2 NPWT and 3 SC participants) exited the trial after completing the maximum 6-month follow-up. A further five participants (two NPWT and three SC) completed less than the 6-month follow-up as they were recruited later into the study. Two participants died during the trial...”

_evidence.notes: The article states “There was no attrition recorded during this trial.” The reported early exits were due to later recruitment, not loss after randomization.
